title: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman traces the decade-long arc of the modern abortion abolition movement from a small SBC church group in Norman, Oklahoma to the Southern Baptist Convention's 2021 Resolution on Abolishing Abortion. The piece argues that abolitionism, after years of clerical rejection, has now become the official position of the largest Christian denomination in the United States.
audiences: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
Abolitionist activists and historians of the movement
Skeptical evangelical clergy unfamiliar with abolitionism

questions.question: What exactly did the SBC's 2021 resolution call for, and how did it characterize pro-life incrementalism?
questions.answer: The resolution called for the immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise and asked the convention to lament its complicity in incremental pro-life regulations that codify when, where, why, and how legal abortion may be obtained. It treated incrementalism as a compromise of God's standard of justice rather than a strategic stepping stone.
questions.quote: the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) adopted a mold-breaking resolution calling for the “immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise,” and further, for the convention to “lament any complicity” in pro-life incrementalism which “compromises God’s holy standard of justice” by establishing “incremental, regulatory guidelines for when, where, why, and how to obtain legal abortion of innocent preborn children.”

questions.question: How did the modern abolitionist movement begin within the SBC, and who sparked it?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists and historians of the movement
questions.answer: It began roughly a decade before the resolution, when an Oklahoma graduate history student (T. Russell Hunter) was tasked with leading a small SBC church discussion on supporting the Pro-Life Movement. Studying 19th-century abolitionists alongside contemporary pro-life messaging convinced him the movement needed reform, and the resulting Abolitionist Society of Norman launched as a ministry within an SBC church.
questions.quote: A decade ago, a small group within a Southern Baptist church in Norman, OK met to discuss supporting the Pro-Life Movement. The task of leading the discussion was assigned to a graduate student in history at the University of Oklahoma.

questions.question: What historical figures shaped the founder's reframing of pro-life work as abolitionism?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: He drew from Christian abolitionists who demanded immediate abolition of the great evils of their day in the name of Christ — Wilberforce, Garrison, and Heyrick — and noticed how little the modern Pro-Life Movement's tactics and messaging resembled theirs.
questions.quote: his historical studies simultaneously led him to the works and biographies of William Wilberforce, William Lloyd Garrison, Elizabeth Heyrick, and other abolitionists who demanded the immediate abolition of the evil of their age in the name of Jesus Christ.

questions.question: Why did the early movement struggle to gain traction with American clergy?
questions.audience: Skeptical evangelical clergy unfamiliar with abolitionism
questions.answer: After internal church frictions led to the disfellowshipping of core members of the Norman society, slander related to the excommunication followed the abolitionists wherever they went, scaring off pastors — even ones who initially praised the movement's gospel-centeredness, like Paul Washer.
questions.quote: However, slander related to the excommunication seemed to follow them everywhere they went, causing pastors they encountered to reject them, with only a few brave exceptions. Some pastors — even prominent ones like Paul Washer — loved the gospel-centeredness of the movement and said so publicly before the slander frightened them away.

questions.question: What was the political breakthrough that gave abolitionists their first foothold in legislation?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: In 2016, after meeting with two abolitionists, Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk became an abolitionist and filed the first bill to abolish abortion immediately in explicit defiance of Roe v. Wade, marking the movement's first legislative beachhead.
questions.quote: In 2016, abolitionists finally got the break they had been working and praying for. After meeting with two abolitionists — one of whom was the aforementioned history student — Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk became an abolitionist and filed the first bill to abolish abortion immediately in explicit defiance of Roe.

questions.question: What event in 2019 finally galvanized Oklahoma Southern Baptists against their own denominational leadership?
questions.answer: On February 21, 2019, the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma published an open letter to every SBC church in the state announcing that the convention would oppose Sen. Silk's abolition bill. The reaction from rank-and-file Baptists was swift: a capitol rally, pastor roundtables, and a flood of complaints to BGCO leadership.
questions.quote: On February 21, 2019, the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma (now called Oklahoma Baptists) proved true what many had chalked up as an abolitionist conspiracy theory, by publishing an open letter to every Southern Baptist church in the state explaining that they, as a convention, were going to oppose Senator Silk’s abolition bill. The response from Southern Baptists in the State was swift and severe.

questions.question: Which pastoral conversion does Silberman call the most stunning, and why does it matter?
questions.audience: Skeptical evangelical clergy unfamiliar with abolitionism
questions.answer: BGCO president Blake Gideon — who had co-authored the 2019 letter opposing immediate abolition — publicly joined the abolitionists in 2020. Silberman frames it as the symbolic moment abolitionists ceased being ecclesiological outcasts and began preaching the cause from pulpits that had once opposed them.
questions.quote: The most stunning pastoral conversion came when BGCO president Blake Gideon, who co-authored the letter opposing immediate abolition in 2019, joined the ranks of the abolitionists in 2020. No longer were abolitionists ecclesiological outcasts.

questions.question: How did the SBC Resolutions Committee initially try to block the abolition resolution, and what made the response especially damning?
questions.answer: The Committee declined the resolution on the grounds that a Hyde Amendment funding resolution (Resolution #3) supposedly already addressed abortion. Yet the same Committee approved a resolution demanding the Chinese government cease its Uyghur genocide "immediately," exposing an inconsistency in calling for prophetic justice abroad while settling for funding language at home.
questions.quote: Yet, the Committee approved Resolution #8 regarding the ongoing genocide of the Uyghur people in China. This resolution, as opposed to Resolution #3, called for the Chinese government to “cease its program of genocide against the Uyghur people immediately” (emphasis mine).

questions.question: What procedural maneuver forced the resolution before the full convention, and how rare is it?
questions.answer: Pastor Bill Ascol motioned to override the Resolutions Committee — a move that requires a two-thirds majority and, by Ascol's account, had not succeeded since at least 1979. More than 80 percent of messengers voted in favor, scheduling the resolution for deliberation the next day.
questions.quote: abolitionist pastor Bill Ascol motioned to force the Committee to bring the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion before the full convention. Overriding the Committee takes a two-thirds majority, which, according to Ascol, had not been successfully accomplished since at least 1979.

questions.question: What floor amendment did Silberman argue rendered article 14 incoherent?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: A messenger added the word "alone" to article 14, so it only rejected pursuing incrementalism by itself. Silberman argues this nullifies the article's logic: if incrementalism truly challenges God's lordship and rejects His call to repent immediately, it cannot coherently be pursued alongside immediatism.
questions.quote: It doesn’t compute that we should pursue both incrementalism and immediatism if incrementalism “challenges God’s Lordship over the heart and the conscience, and rejects His call to repent of sin completely and immediately.”

questions.question: Despite the article 14 amendment, why does Silberman insist the resolution still represents an immediatist victory?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists and historians of the movement
questions.answer: He notes that more than a dozen other places in the resolution describe incrementalism as "appalling" and "God-hating partiality" to be "lamented," so the document's overall posture remains unmistakably immediatist — a point even mainstream outlets like The Tennessean and the New York Times noted in their coverage.
questions.quote: While article 14 no longer makes sense, there are more than a dozen other places in the resolution where incrementalism is referred to as “appalling” and “God-hating partiality” to be “lamented.” Immediatism remains the perfectly clear position of the resolution.

questions.question: What does Silberman predict about SBC and ERLC leaders' efforts to walk the resolution back?
questions.answer: He predicts their repudiation efforts will fail, comparing them to trying to stop the wind by grabbing it, and frames abolitionism as the only biblical response to abortion — a truth he says the broader Church is finally grasping.
questions.quote: Since the passage of the resolution, pro-life SBC and Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission leaders have worked to repudiate the resolution; but it will be as if they were trying to stop the wind by grabbing hold of it.

related.title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
related.reason: Silberman's same-day report from Nashville covering the procedural override and amendment debates analyzed in this retrospective.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Documents the 2019 BGCO open-letter episode that Silberman cites as the watershed moment for Oklahoma Baptists turning toward abolitionism.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: Covers the 2019 state-level resolution Silberman references as a precursor showing pastors connecting with Oklahoma abolitionist organizations.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
related.reason: Continues the Oklahoma Baptist trajectory the article narrates, showing the state convention reinforcing immediatism after the SBC's 2021 vote.

related.title: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
related.reason: Captures the ERLC-aligned hostility toward abolitionism that Silberman foreshadows when he warns about post-passage repudiation efforts.

related.title: Tom Ascol Nominated For Southern Baptist Convention President
related.reason: Follows the Ascol family's escalating leadership in SBC abolitionist politics that began with Bill Ascol's procedural motion described here.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Lays out the immediatism-vs-incrementalism theological argument that drove the article 14 amendment dispute Silberman dissects.
